mn121:1.2: At one time the _Bhagavā_ was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in the Eastern Park, in Migāra’s Mother’s Mansion.
mn121:10.1: Again, Ānanda, a _bhikkhu_, without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*], without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither _saññā_ nor non-_saññā_ [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*], attends to singleness dependent on the mind’s _samādhi_ without _nimitta_ [*animittaṁ cetosamādhiṁ*].
mn121:10.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that mind’s _samādhi_ without _nimitta_ [*animitte cetosamādhimhi*], becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:10.4: ‘Whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*], they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither _saññā_ nor non-_saññā_ [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*], they are not present here. There is only this amount of disturbance, namely—
mn121:10.5: this very body, with its dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanikaṁ*], conditioned by life.’
mn121:10.6: He understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*]’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither _saññā_ nor non-_saññā_ [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*]’; he understands: ‘Yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:10.7: this very body, with its dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanikaṁ*], conditioned by life.’
mn121:10.8: So whatever is not there, he sees that as empty; but whatever remains there, he understands: ‘This is present.’
mn121:10.9: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him a genuine, undistorted, pure entry into emptiness.
mn121:11.1: Again, Ānanda, a _bhikkhu_, without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*], without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither _saññā_ nor non-_saññā_ [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*], attends to singleness dependent on the mind’s _samādhi_ without _nimitta_ [*animittaṁ cetosamādhiṁ*].
mn121:11.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that mind’s _samādhi_ without _nimitta_ [*animitte cetosamādhimhi*], becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:11.4: ‘This mind’s _samādhi_ without _nimitta_ [*animitto cetosamādhi*] too is deliberately _saṅkhata_ [*abhisaṅkhato*], deliberately intended [*abhisañcetayito*].’
mn121:11.5: He understands: ‘But whatever is in any way deliberately _saṅkhata_ [*abhisaṅkhataṁ*], deliberately intended [*abhisañcetayitaṁ*], that is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], of a nature to cease [*nirodhadhammaṁ*].’
mn121:11.6: For one knowing thus, seeing thus, the mind [*cittaṁ*] is liberated from the _āsava_ of sensuality [*kāmāsavā*], the _āsava_ of _bhava_ [*bhavāsavā*], and the _āsava_ of blindness [*avijjāsavā*].
mn121:11.7: When it is liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn121:11.8: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn121:12.2: ‘Whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the _āsava_ of sensuality [*kāmāsavaṁ*], they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the _āsava_ of _bhava_ [*bhavāsavaṁ*], they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the _āsava_ of blindness [*avijjāsavaṁ*], they are not present here. There is only this amount of disturbance, namely—
mn121:12.3: this very body, with its dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanikaṁ*], conditioned by life.’
mn121:12.4: He understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the _āsava_ of sensuality [*kāmāsavena*]’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the _āsava_ of _bhava_ [*bhavāsavena*]’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the _āsava_ of blindness [*avijjāsavena*]’; he understands: ‘Yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:12.5: this very body, with its dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanikaṁ*], conditioned by life.’
mn121:12.6: So whatever is not there, he sees that as empty; but whatever remains there, he understands: ‘This is present.’
mn121:12.7: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him a genuine, undistorted, pure, supreme, unsurpassed entry into emptiness.
mn121:13.1: Indeed, Ānanda, whatever ascetics or brahmins in the past dwelt having entered upon the pure, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness, all of them dwelt having entered upon this very pure, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness.
mn121:13.2: Indeed, Ānanda, whatever ascetics or brahmins in the future will dwell having entered upon the pure, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness, all of them will dwell having entered upon this very pure, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness.
mn121:13.3: Indeed, Ānanda, whatever ascetics or brahmins at present dwell having entered upon the pure, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness, all of them dwell having entered upon this very pure, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness.
mn121:13.4: Therefore, Ānanda, you should train thus: ‘We will dwell having entered upon the pure, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness’—
mn121:13.5: that is how you should train, Ānanda.”ti.
mn121:13.7: Satisfied, Venerable Ānanda delighted in the _Bhagavā_’s words.
mn121:13.8: The first, The Shorter Discourse on Emptiness, is finished.
mn121:2.1: Then Venerable Ānanda, in the late afternoon, rose from seclusion and went to the _Bhagavā_. Having approached the _Bhagavā_, he paid homage to him and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Venerable Ānanda said this to the _Bhagavā_:
mn121:3.1: “At one time, *bhante*, the _Bhagavā_ was dwelling among the Sakyans at a town of the Sakyans named Nagaraka.
mn121:3.2: There, *bhante*, I heard this in the presence of the _Bhagavā_, received it in the presence of the _Bhagavā_:
mn121:3.3: ‘Ānanda, these days I often dwell in emptiness.’
mn121:3.4: Was this, *bhante*, well heard by me, well grasped, well attended to, and well retained?”
mn121:3.5: “Indeed, Ānanda, that was well heard by you, well grasped, well attended to, and well retained.
mn121:3.6: In the past too, Ānanda, and now too, I often dwell in emptiness.
mn121:4.1: Just as, Ānanda, this Migāra’s Mother’s Mansion is empty of elephants, cattle, horses, and mares; empty of gold and silver; empty of gatherings of women and men; yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:4.10: So whatever is not there, he sees that as empty; but whatever remains there, he understands: ‘This is present.’
mn121:4.11: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him a genuine, undistorted, pure entry into emptiness.
mn121:4.3: just so, Ānanda, a _bhikkhu_, without attending to the perception of village, without attending to the perception of people, attends to singleness dependent on the perception of wilderness.
mn121:4.4: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that perception of wilderness, becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:4.6: ‘Whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of village, they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of people, they are not present here. There is only this amount of disturbance, namely—
mn121:4.7: singleness dependent on the perception of wilderness.’
mn121:4.8: He understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of village’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of people’; he understands: ‘Yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:4.9: singleness dependent on the perception of wilderness.’
mn121:5.1: Again, Ānanda, a _bhikkhu_, without attending to the perception of people, without attending to the perception of wilderness, attends to singleness dependent on the perception of earth.
mn121:5.10: singleness dependent on the perception of earth.’
mn121:5.11: So whatever is not there, he sees that as empty; but whatever remains there, he understands: ‘This is present.’
mn121:5.12: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him a genuine, undistorted, pure entry into emptiness.
mn121:5.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that perception of earth, becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:5.3: Just as, Ānanda, a bull’s hide, well stretched with a hundred pegs, is rid of wrinkles;
mn121:5.4: just so, Ānanda, whatever in this earth is uneven ground, river ravines, places of stumps and thorns, rugged mountain terrain — without attending to all that, a _bhikkhu_ attends to singleness dependent on the perception of earth.
mn121:5.5: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that perception of earth, becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:5.7: ‘Whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of people, they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of wilderness, they are not present here. There is only this amount of disturbance, namely—
mn121:5.8: singleness dependent on the perception of earth.’
mn121:5.9: He understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of people’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of wilderness’; he understands: ‘Yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:6.1: Again, Ānanda, a _bhikkhu_, without attending to the perception of wilderness, without attending to the perception of earth, attends to singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*].
mn121:6.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*], becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:6.4: ‘Whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of wilderness, they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of earth, they are not present here. There is only this amount of disturbance, namely—
mn121:6.5: singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*].’
mn121:6.6: He understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of wilderness’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of earth’; he understands: ‘Yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:6.7: singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*].’
mn121:6.8: So whatever is not there, he sees that as empty; but whatever remains there, he understands: ‘This is present.’
mn121:6.9: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him a genuine, undistorted, pure entry into emptiness.
mn121:7.1: Again, Ānanda, a _bhikkhu_, without attending to the perception of earth, without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*], attends to singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*].
mn121:7.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*], becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:7.4: ‘Whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of earth, they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*], they are not present here. There is only this amount of disturbance, namely—
mn121:7.5: singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*].’
mn121:7.6: He understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of earth’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*]’; he understands: ‘Yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:7.7: singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*].’
mn121:7.8: So whatever is not there, he sees that as empty; but whatever remains there, he understands: ‘This is present.’
mn121:7.9: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him a genuine, undistorted, pure entry into emptiness.
mn121:8.1: Again, Ānanda, a _bhikkhu_, without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*], without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*], attends to singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*].
mn121:8.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*], becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:8.4: ‘Whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*], they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*], they are not present here. There is only this amount of disturbance, namely—
mn121:8.5: singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*].’
mn121:8.6: He understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*]’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*]’; he understands: ‘Yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:8.7: singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*].’
mn121:8.8: So whatever is not there, he sees that as empty; but whatever remains there, he understands: ‘This is present.’
mn121:8.9: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him a genuine, undistorted, pure entry into emptiness.
mn121:9.1: Again, Ānanda, a _bhikkhu_, without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*], without attending to the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*], attends to singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither _saññā_ nor non-_saññā_ [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*].
mn121:9.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither _saññā_ nor non-_saññā_ [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*], becomes clear, becomes steady, and settles on it.
mn121:9.4: ‘Whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*], they are not present here; whatever disturbances would exist dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*], they are not present here. There is only this amount of disturbance, namely—
mn121:9.5: singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither _saññā_ nor non-_saññā_ [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*].’
mn121:9.6: He understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*]’; he understands: ‘This perceptual state is empty of the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*]’; he understands: ‘Yet there is this not-empty thing, namely—
mn121:9.7: singleness dependent on the perception of the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither _saññā_ nor non-_saññā_ [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*].’
mn121:9.8: So whatever is not there, he sees that as empty; but whatever remains there, he understands: ‘This is present.’
mn121:9.9: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him a genuine, undistorted, pure entry into emptiness.
